The San Diego Museum of Man, dba Museum of Us, sits on the unceded ancestral homelands of the Kumeyaay Nation, in San Diegos Balboa Park. The Museum of Us extends its respect and gratitude to the Kumeyaay peoples who have lived here for millennia. The Museum was founded in 1915, as part of the citys Panama-California Exposition. On August 2, 2020, the Museum announced its change of name to Museum of Us. This new name is a registered Doing Business As until a full legal change is executed. At its founding, the Museum was the epitome of an early 20th century institution of its kind; a place defined by its ethnographic collections and their traditional western European academic display. In January 2012, the Board of Directors adopted a three-year strategic plan that identified a new mission: Inspiring human connections by exploring the human experience. The institutional goal for the Museum is to be San Diegos dynamic place to go to learn from each other, reflect on our place in the world

Georgia Transplant Foundation ("GTF") exists to bridge the financial and emotional gaps faced by organ transplant candidates, recipients, and their families throughout Georgia. In 2024, GTF continued to provide a comprehensive portfolio of programs, including financial assistance, educational initiatives, mentoring and support services, and return-to-work readiness through the JumpStart Program. GTF also hosted multiple community-based events, including the Trends in Transplant Conferences in four cities, to promote education, peer connection, and empowerment among those impacted by transplantation. With a strong focus on sustainability and equity, GTF worked closely with transplant centers, social workers, and community partners to reach patients in both urban and rural areas, ensuring that life-saving support was accessible regardless of income or geography. In 2024, GTF's patient programs and services were utilized by 1,899 Georgians impacted by transplantation.

Incentives to recruit the teachers include signing bonuses, relocation fees, affordable housing, and scholarships. TLM is focused on developing a strong instructional pedagogy with a culturally responsive lens. Teachers will be provided additional professional develop by TLM to improve instructional practices. Teachers remaining in their school and/or district will receive a retention bonus each year. It's not enough to just be able to recruit and develop great teachers if we don't invest in filling the needs of those teachers and making sure that we retain them in our schools.

organization_name: Habitat for Humanity of High Point
mission_description: Habitat for Humanity of High Point, Archdale and Trinity is an ecumenical Christian movement whose goal is to build safe, decent, affordable homes for our neighbors in need. Through conscience and action, Habitat utilizes volunteer labor and donations, as well as the help of the homeowner, to offer a long-term solution to the housing needs of low-income families in our community. Qualifying families partnered with Habitat purchase their homes with a 25-year, no interest mortgage. The family's mortgage payment goes back to Habitat allowing for construction of more houses. Since 1987, Habitat for Humanity of High Point, Archdale and Trinity has helped provide homes with and for working, low-income families that cannot qualify for financing in the current market. In addition to "sweat equity" provided by the families who partner with Habitat, building and revitalization efforts are also made possible through partnerships with local churches, businesses and civic organizations. Creighton Community Foundation's (CCF) core program "Community Works" leverages neighborhood schools to support vibrant communities in the poorest parts of urban phoenix, where neighborhoods suffer from poverty driven disadvantage and disengagement. in these neighborhoods, today's children will spend 91% of their lives between 0 and 18 years of age outside school hallways. Vibrant supportive communities today have a determinative influence on child outcomes, and tomorrow's neighborhoods. What does it take to change an isolated, disengaged community into one filled with adult supportive relationships and a community of hope and inspiration? We leverage best practices in community transformation, centered around relocation, redistribution, and reconciliation by embedding a school connected worker in our poorest neighborhoods, living 7x24 in residence, working in child and community relationship, and building bridges with key community partners and resources. The change is here already.

The world is grappling with complex problems, including climate change, war, poverty, and ongoing human rights violations. An abundance of research indicates that when women participate equitably in decision-making, solutions to problems are more likely to impact all and last longer. To address gender inequality, SHE-CAN builds female leadership by empowering low-opportunity women from post-conflict countries to attend U.S. colleges and return home with leadership skills that allow them to positively influence their home communities and nations. With combined power and collective influence, SHE-CAN scholars create change that reverberates globally.

HAVE A HEART FOUNDATION, INC. (HAVE A HEART) IS A MEDICAL CLINIC THAT PROVIDES HIGH QUALITY CARDIOVASCULAR CARE TO ADULT PATIENTS IN AND AROUND THE KENTUCKIANA AREA REGARDLESS OF THEIR INSURANCE STATUS OR ABILITY TO PAY. PATIENTS AT OR BELOW 200% OF THE FEDERAL POVERTY LEVEL RECEIVE CARE AT NO COST. HAVE A HEART CONDUCTS FREE CARDIOVASCULAR SCREENINGS IN THE UNDERSERVED COMMUNITIES AND REFERS PATIENTS WITH INTERMEDIATE TO HIGH RISK TO THE CLINIC FOR CARE. THESE PATIENTS ALONG WITH OTHER CLINIC PATIENTS AT INTERMEDIATE TO HIGH RISK ARE ALSO REFERRED TO THE HAVE A HEART HEALTH EQUITY CENTER. THE NURSE EDUCATOR IN THE HEALTH EQUITY CENTER WORKS WITH THESE PATIENTS TO IMPROVE THEIR OVERALL QUALITY OF LIFE. THEY ASSESS THE SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH AND DEVELOP A PLAN WITH THE PATIENT TO IMPROVE THEIR OVERALL HEALTH AND WELL-BEING. REFERRALS ARE MADE TO OTHER COMMUNITY RESOURCES AS NEEDED. PATIENT DATA IS COLLECTED AND TRACKED TO MEASURE OVERALL IMPROVEMENTS.

Black Men Teach (BMT) recruits, prepares, places, and retains Black male teachers in elementary schools by building a robust pathway to the teaching profession, beginning in high school and continuing to support men through college and into their teaching career. BMT began in 2018 based on research that told us that a diverse teacher corps improves student outcomes for all children, especially children of color. Black students in elementary school who have at least one Black male teacher are 29% less likely to drop out of school. Those who have two Black male teachers are 32% more likely to enroll in post-secondary education. Since its start in 2018, BMT has gone from serving 12 Black men in 2020, to nearly 100 in fiscal 2024. To support our rapid ramp up in programming, our staffing and budgeted spend are ramping up. We are developing a coalition of schools, school districts, teacher training and scholarship programs, and community organizations to achieve our objective.

WITH 2.2 BILLION PEOPLE IN THE WORLD STILL WITHOUT ACCESS TO SAFE WATER, THEY HAD A VISION FOR AN ORGANIZATION THAT COULD SOLVE THE PROBLEM FASTER. IN 2020, WATER MISSION TURNED THAT VISION INTO REALITY BY FOUNDING GLOBAL WATER CENTER (GWC).AT GWC, WE BELIEVE EVERYONE DESERVES ACCESS TO SAFELY MANAGED WATER. WITHOUT SUSTAINABLE WATER SYSTEMS, THIS GOAL REMAINS OUT OF REACH. THAT IS WHY GWC FOCUSES ON CAPACITY DEVELOPMENT, TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND COLLABORATION, EQUIPPING LEADERS TO CREATE SUSTAINABLE SOLUTIONS AT THE SCALE NEEDED TO END THE GLOBAL WATER CRISIS.
